title: Andersen expects $980M-$1B 2026 revenue while raising inorganic revenue outlook to $55M
content:
Earnings Call Insights: Andersen Group Inc. (ANDG) Q1 2026

MANAGEMENT VIEW

 	* Mark Vorsatz (Chairman & CEO) said the company delivered “a very solid first quarter,” reporting revenue “a little under $241 million” with “15.7%” growth, and emphasized productivity and pricing progress, including “revenue per professional...12.7%.”
 	* Mark Vorsatz (Chairman & CEO) highlighted profitability and the drag from newer practices: “On the adjusted EBITDA numbers, we came in at around $72.3 million...the margin was 30%,” adding that this “includes about a $7.4 million loss in Global Mobility and Consulting,” and that “we’re going to lose money in both of those practices this year.”
 	* Neal Livingstone (Chief Financial Officer) pointed to the new pricing lever: “the 3% tech charge...introduced for client contracts signed in the first quarter of 2026...has met, if not exceeded our internal expectations, and it will provide a meaningful source of incremental revenue for 2026.”

OUTLOOK

 	* Neal Livingstone (Chief Financial Officer) guided Q2 2026 revenue to “$190 million to $205 million,” and said, “We are anticipating a net loss for the quarter and negative EPS...due to seasonality and principally the aforementioned non-cash equity-based compensation expenses.”
 	* Neal Livingstone (Chief Financial Officer) updated full-year 2026 expectations to revenue of “$980 million to $1 billion,” while stating, “We are anticipating positive net income and EPS for the full year,” and guiding adjusted EBITDA of “$225 million to $250 million” with margin “23% to 25%.”
 	* Neal Livingstone (Chief Financial Officer) said the company “closed several acquisitions in the second quarter, approximately one quarter ahead of schedule,” and “rais[ed] our full year inorganic revenue guidance from $33 million to $55 million,” with updates to acquisition impacts coming with Q2 results.

FINANCIAL RESULTS

 	* Neal Livingstone (Chief Financial Officer) reported Q1 2026 revenue of “$240.7 million,” noting there was “no inorganic or M&A revenue recorded in the first quarter of 2026.”
 	* Neal Livingstone (Chief Financial Officer) said GAAP net income was “$17.7 million,” and attributed the year-over-year decline primarily to “$41.2 million of non-cash equity-based compensation expense,” plus “interest expense increased $6 million” and “transaction costs increased by $2.6 million...in support of the firm’s ongoing inorganic expansion plans.”
 	* Neal Livingstone (Chief Financial Officer) reported adjusted EBITDA of “$72.3 million” and said the adjusted EBITDA margin was “30%,” while also stating adjusted net income was “$62.9 million.”
 	* Neal Livingstone (Chief Financial Officer) described liquidity and leverage: “As of March 31, 2026,” cash and cash equivalents were “approximately $207 million,” and “At the end of the quarter, the firm had no third-party debt.”

Q&A

 	* Mark Marcon, Baird: asked about whether proposed wealth and income tax initiatives were contributing to Private Client Services momentum; Mark Vorsatz (Chairman & CEO): “I don’t think...those are baked into the numbers for the first quarter at all,” adding that “the bigger issue on the PCS practice is we continue to add more clients and larger clients.”
 	* Mark Marcon, Baird: asked whether the January price increase met expectations; Mark Vorsatz (Chairman & CEO): “Yes, I think pretty much,” and added, “for the balance of the year, we’ll see much greater lift...on the productivity side.”
 	* Mark Marcon, Baird: asked what drives the implied acceleration in full-year guidance and whether Q2 includes acquisition revenue; Mark Vorsatz (Chairman & CEO): “we have some very modest revenue included in the second quarter numbers...a little less than $7 million,” and said the higher inorganic contribution is “primarily almost exclusively in the second half of the year.”
 	* Faiza Alwy, Deutsche Bank: asked whether margin expansion reflects M&A mix or organic progress and about deal structure; Mark Vorsatz (Chairman & CEO): “all the margin expansion for the first quarter is 100% organic,” and said acquisition margins “may slide a little bit in the short term” due to integration.
 	* Toni Kaplan, Morgan Stanley: asked about AI-driven monetization shifting away from hourly rates and sought milestones; Mark Vorsatz (Chairman & CEO): “there are going to be components of both time and materials and components of an increasing amount on fixed fees,” but on targets said, “it’s a little premature to be in a position to give you information on that.”
 	* Tobey Sommer, Truist: asked about acquisition runway and profitability timing for Global Mobility and Consulting; Mark Vorsatz (Chairman & CEO): “if I had 100% capacity, I could spend the next 3 years just working on deals,” and said Consulting is “more likely...in the black second half of next year,” while Global Mobility is “probably 2028 is educated guesswork.”
 	* Jun-Yi Xie, Wells Fargo: asked why full-year guidance didn’t reflect more of Q1 outperformance excluding inorganic changes; Neal Livingstone (Chief Financial Officer): “we’re taking the overall organic up considerably, about 2.5% from where we were in the original projection.”
 	* Andrew Nicholas, William Blair: asked about target prioritization and Consulting momentum; Mark Vorsatz (Chairman & CEO): “you’ll see us continue to add practices that have existing management,” and said there are “24 affiliates in the United States in Consulting,” with a plan to start the buildout “with the United States.”

SENTIMENT ANALYSIS

 	* Analysts’ tone was slightly positive, with repeated questions probing sustainability of growth drivers, the acquisition pipeline and economics, and how AI could alter pricing and operating model, including requests for “targets or milestones” (Toni Kaplan, Morgan Stanley).
 	* Management’s tone was positive and execution-focused in prepared remarks, emphasizing guidance and expansion, including Neal Livingstone (Chief Financial Officer) stating, “we are providing updated guidance,” and Mark Vorsatz (Chairman & CEO) emphasizing “we have a lot of room for improvement.”
 	* Compared with Q4 2025, management language shifted from expecting a full-year 2026 net loss (prior call) to Neal Livingstone (Chief Financial Officer) now stating, “We are anticipating positive net income and EPS for the full year,” while maintaining cautious language on AI specifics: “it’s a little premature.”

QUARTER-OVER-QUARTER COMPARISON

 	* Guidance framing changed from Q4 2025’s full-year revenue outlook of “$955 million to $970 million” and expected “net loss in 2026,” to Q1 2026’s higher revenue range of “$980 million to $1 billion” and “positive net income and EPS for the full year” (Neal Livingstone, Chief Financial Officer).
 	* Acquisition expectations advanced from Q4 2025 describing $33 million of 2026 inorganic revenue and early deal signings, to Q1 2026 saying deals were “closed...one quarter ahead of schedule” and raising inorganic guidance “from $33 million to $55 million” (Neal Livingstone, Chief Financial Officer).
 	* AI moved from Q4 2025’s “two more pilots...in May and June” to Q1 2026’s rollout beginning: “we actually started the rollout of our internal training...in increments of 500 people” (Mark Vorsatz, Chairman & CEO).

RISKS AND CONCERNS

 	* Neal Livingstone (Chief Financial Officer) flagged near-term earnings volatility: Q2 is expected to show “a net loss...and negative EPS” due to “seasonality” and “non-cash equity-based compensation expenses.”
 	* Mark Vorsatz (Chairman & CEO) acknowledged integration execution risk: on acquisitions, “there’ll be some level of transition costs,” and “we’ve got to get groups integrated.”
 	* Mark Vorsatz (Chairman & CEO) reiterated that Global Mobility and Consulting are expected to remain losses in 2026: “we’re going to lose money in both of those practices this year,” framing it as investment tied to expansion.

FINAL TAKEAWAY

Management positioned Q1 as a broad-based organic growth quarter with improved productivity and pricing, while using the results to raise full-year expectations to $980 million to $1 billion in revenue and to lift inorganic revenue guidance to $55 million following Q2 deal closings. The company also guided to a seasonally weak Q2 with an expected net loss and negative EPS, while stressing balance sheet liquidity and “no third-party debt,” and describing AI as an efficiency and service-delivery initiative that is underway through internal training but not yet ready for specific milestone commitments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/andg/earnings/transcripts]

MORE ON ANDERSEN GROUP INC.

 	* Andersen Group Inc. (ANDG) Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript [https://seekingalpha.com/article/4903429-andersen-group-inc-andg-q1-2026-earnings-call-transcript]
 	* Andersen Group Inc. 2026 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation [https://seekingalpha.com/article/4903546-andersen-group-inc-2026-q1-results-earnings-call-presentation]

Operations: The company's revenue is derived from its operations at Wharf ($339.18 million), Palmarejo ($473.76 million), Rochester ($458.04 million), Kensington ($377.71 million), and Las Chispas ($421.43 million).

Estimated Discount To Fair Value: 18%

Coeur Mining's stock is trading 18% below its estimated fair value of US$22.33, with a current price of US$18.31, indicating potential undervaluation based on cash flows. Despite recent shareholder dilution, Coeur's earnings surged significantly last year and are expected to grow at 24.4% annually over the next three years, outpacing the broader U.S. market growth rate. The company recently secured a $1 billion credit facility for working capital and general corporate purposes.

According our earnings growth report, there's an indication that Coeur Mining might be ready to expand. Take a closer look at Coeur Mining's balance sheet health here in our report.CDE Discounted Cash Flow as at Mar 2026

title: Andersen Group (ANDG) Jumps 27.9% on Double-Digit Revenue Growth Hopes
content:
Andersen Group Inc. (NYSE:ANDG) is one of the 10 Stocks Gaining Momentum Fast.

Andersen Group soared by 27.90 percent week-on-week, as investors cheered the company’s highly optimistic outlook for this year, with revenues targeted to grow by double digits.

In its earnings call earlier in the week, Andersen Group Inc. (NYSE:ANDG) said that it is gunning for a revenue growth of 14 to 15 percent this year, at $955 million to $970 million; as well as adjusted EBITDA growth of 22 to 23 percent to a range of $213 million to $220 million.

Photo by Karolina Grabowska on Pexels

“We are entering 2026 with strong momentum and a clear focus on disciplined growth—investing in the expansion of our platform, integrating high-quality firms across key markets, and deploying technology, automation, and AI to enhance efficiency and scale our services. These investments position us to further strengthen our market leadership while driving sustained revenue growth and increased profitability over time,” Andersen Group Inc. (NYSE:ANDG) Chairman and CEO Mark Vorsatz said.

Last year, Andersen Group Inc. (NYSE:ANDG) grew its revenues by 14.6 percent to $838.69 million from $731.59 million in 2024.

However, it swung to a net loss of $130.17 million from a $134.8 million net income year-on-year.

In the fourth quarter alone, net loss widened by 1,919 percent to $195.87 million from $9.7 million, while revenues jumped by 19 percent to $170 million from $142 million.

While we acknowledge the potential of ANDG as an investment, we believe certain AI stocks offer greater upside potential and carry less downside risk. If you're looking for an extremely undervalued AI stock that also stands to benefit significantly from Trump-era tariffs and the onshoring trend, see our free report on the best short-term AI stock.

title: Why Andersen Group Stock Crushed it Today
content:
A record-breaking fourth quarter was the catalyst behind the surge in Andersen Group(NYSE: ANDG) stock on Wednesday. Investors were obviously impressed with the tax and financial advisory company's performance in that frame, as they bid its stock up by almost 14% across the Hump Day trading session.

A veteran operator in a new corporate form

For the quarter, Andersen grew revenue by almost 20% year over year to slightly over $170 million. On the bottom line, equity restructuring costs arising from its transformation from a private partnership to a publicly traded company totaled more than $193 million. That drove it far into the red, with a headline net loss of over $193 million ($0.22 per share), from the year-ago deficit of under $10 million.

Will AI create the world's first trillionaire? Our team just released a report on the one little-known company, called an "Indispensable Monopoly" providing the critical technology Nvidia and Intel both need. Continue »Image source: Getty Images.

On an operational basis, throughout 2025, Andersen said it provided services to more than 12,350 client groups in its home market in the U.S. That meant a growth rate of nearly 6% from the 2024 level. Of these, 687 contributed more than $250,000 in annual revenue, compared with 629 the year before.

Andersen quoted CEO Mark Vorsatz as saying the fourth quarter "capped a record year for the firm, and underscores the strength of our global, multi-dimensional platform and the continued demand for high-value advisory services."

Double-digit growth to repeat?

Vorsatz indicated that he and his team expect that momentum to continue. Sure enough, Andersen proffered guidance of $955 million to $970 million in revenue for the full year 2026, representing at least 14% growth over the previous year. The company did not provide a net income forecast.

Andersen has a long, if somewhat tangled, history as a high-end advisor on tax and other financial matters. All things being equal, with a frothy economy, it should be able to tap into more business and keep the growth train running. While not the most exciting stock, it does feel like a safe play during relatively prosperous times.
